title: How Redshift Proves the Universe Is Stretching

lesson.learn: Redshift is the stretching of light toward longer wavelengths. When a galaxy moves away from us, the light it emits gets shifted toward the red end of the spectrum, just as a sound changes pitch when its source moves. In astronomy, redshift became one of the key clues that the universe is expanding. Distant galaxies are not simply sitting still while we move past them. Space itself is stretching, and that stretching carries their light with it. The effect is not limited to motion through space. On cosmic scales, the expansion of space changes the wavelength of light as it travels. That is why very distant objects have stronger redshifts than nearby ones. Measuring redshift lets astronomers estimate distance, map galaxy distribution, and reconstruct the history of the expanding universe.
lesson.simpler: Light from distant galaxies gets stretched to redder wavelengths, and that tells us the universe itself is expanding.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Redshift is one of astronomy's most important clues because it turns light into a record of cosmic expansion, showing that the universe is not static but growing larger over time.

learnImageCredit.creator: Georg Wiora ( Dr. Schorsch ) created this image from the original JPG.

lesson.deeperDive: Redshift is measured by comparing known atomic spectral lines with what astronomers actually see. If those lines are displaced toward longer wavelengths, the source is receding or the fabric of space has stretched while the light was in transit. This is how Edwin Hubble and later astronomers built the evidence that distant galaxies are moving away from us. The greater the redshift, the farther away and earlier in cosmic history the source usually is. Redshift also appears in other forms, such as gravitational redshift, where light loses energy climbing out of a strong gravitational field. But the cosmological version is the one that tells the big story: the universe is expanding. By combining redshift with distance measurements, astronomers can trace how expansion changed over time and connect observations to the Big Bang model, dark energy, and the large-scale structure of the cosmos.
lesson.funFact: The farther away a galaxy is, the more its light is usually stretched toward the red end of the spectrum by the time it reaches us.

Wikimedia Commons image metadata for redshift illustrations.

people: Edwin Hubble - Helped establish the relation between galaxy distance and recession.
Astronomers - Measure spectra to determine redshift and cosmic distance.
Spectroscopists - Identify the atomic lines used for redshift measurements.
Cosmologists - Use redshift to reconstruct the universe's expansion history.
places: Distant galaxies - The main sources used to measure redshift.
Spectrographs - Instruments that split light into measurable wavelengths.
The expanding universe - The setting in which wavelength stretching occurs.
The Milky Way - Our local reference point for comparing cosmic motion.
years: 1920s - Hubble's observations link distance and redshift.
1960s - Quasar redshifts extend the cosmic picture to extreme distances.
1990s - Redshift surveys map the large-scale galaxy distribution.
2000s-2020s - Precision cosmology uses redshift to study dark energy.
concepts: Spectral line - A specific wavelength fingerprint from an atom or molecule.
Cosmological redshift - Wavelength stretching caused by expanding space.
Recession velocity - The apparent speed at which distant objects move away.
Distance ladder - The chain of methods astronomers use to estimate cosmic scale.
